Angola.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and climate science capacity-building in Angola** through data-driven agroclimatic analysis and negotiator training. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** developed high-resolution agroclimatic indices (e.g., SPEI, ETo, heatwave metrics) for maize, beans, soybeans, and coffee to assess water balance and crop resilience under ENSO variability (doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX).
- **CGIAR’s AGNES program** strengthened African negotiators’ technical expertise in climate attribution science to link emissions, extremes, and impacts—critical for Angola’s climate negotiations (10568/178443).
Angola.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** generated crop-specific agroclimatic indices (dry/wet spells, heatwaves, SPEI) to inform climate-resilient agricultural planning for Angola’s key crops (doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX).
• **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** trained African negotiators—including Angolan representatives—in attribution science to enhance adaptation policy framing (10568/178443).
Angola.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES program** advanced climate attribution methods to quantify industrialized vs. developing nations’ roles in driving climate extremes, supporting mitigation negotiations (10568/178443).
Angola.Water: • **CGIAR researchers** created a 0.05°-resolution agroclimatic dataset (ETo, SPEI, water balance indices) for Angola’s agricultural regions to assess drought/wet-spell risks (doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX).

Argentina.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and greenhouse gas mitigation** in **Argentina** through **data-driven innovation and emission research**. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) program** advanced **digital agronomy tools** to enhance yields and climate-resilient decision-making for farmers across Latin America, including Argentina (10568/121856).
- CGIAR researchers **measured nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions** from urine patches in degraded and improved pastures, contributing to GHG mitigation strategies in Argentina and other LAC countries (doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ).
Argentina.Adaptation: • CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) program** developed **open, data-driven agronomic tools** (e.g., Digital Climate-Smart Agriculture, D-CSA) to improve yields and support climate-informed decision-making for over 100,000 farmers in Argentina and 7 other LAC nations (10568/121856).
Argentina.Mitigation: • CGIAR researchers **compiled a database of nitrous oxide (N₂O) fluxes** from urine patches in Argentina’s pastures, analyzing soil nitrogen content and emission factors to inform GHG mitigation strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ).
Argentina.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Australia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food systems innovation in Australia** through modeling advancements and strategic policy support. Specifically:
- **APSIM/ORYZA2000 collaboration** enhanced water-use efficiency in rice-based cropping systems by simulating anaerobic/aerobic soil transitions (Source A).
- **CCAFS** facilitated multi-year climate adaptation plans for APEC economies, including Australia, via stakeholder consultations (Source C).
Australia.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **APSIM modeling team** integrated ORYZA2000 to simulate transitions between flooded/non-flooded soil conditions, addressing water shortages and climate-driven resource constraints in rice systems (doi:10.7910/DVN/23782).
• **CCAFS (CIAT, FP2, RPL SEA)** supported Australia’s APEC economies in adopting a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, coordinating stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (10568/121197).
• **Dr. Megan Clark (Commission on Sustainable Agriculture and Climate Change)** highlighted CGIAR’s role in analyzing climate change impacts on Australian food production systems (10568/10486).
Australia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Australia.Water: • CGIAR’s **APSIM model** was upgraded to simulate **soil water dynamics under anaerobic/aerobic conditions**, improving water-use efficiency and floodwater management in rice-based cropping systems (doi:10.7910/DVN/23782).

Bangladesh.narrative:
CGIAR’s **TAFSSA (Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia)** initiative prioritized **climate-resilient agrifood systems in Bangladesh** through district-level, gender-integrated assessments. Specifically:
- TAFSSA conducted **multi-stakeholder household and market surveys** to evaluate climate risk responses, natural resource management, and livelihood resilience in Rangpur and Rajshahi districts (Sources: **doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD**, **doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ**, **doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH**, **doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR**).
Bangladesh.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s TAFSSA **assessed climate risk responses and adaptation strategies** among rural households in Bangladesh, focusing on agricultural land use, crop/livestock diversification, and asset-based resilience in Rangpur and Rajshahi districts (doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD).
• The initiative **documented gender-differentiated climate adaptation practices** through male, female, and adolescent interviews, linking farm production, market access, and social safety nets to adaptive capacity (doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ).
• TAFSSA **evaluated multi-year climate resilience frameworks** via district-level surveys, integrating climate risk data with natural resource management (doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR).
Bangladesh.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Bangladesh.Water:
• CGIAR’s TAFSSA **analyzed water access and natural resource management** as critical components of climate adaptation, with female-led interviews highlighting household water security in Rangpur and Rajshahi (doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ).
• The initiative **linked water resource management to agrifood system resilience**, assessing its role in climate risk responses through district-level assessments (doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH).

- **CCAFS** advised the **Central America Agricultural Council** on **GHG inventories and mitigation practices**, delivering targeted training to ministries on agricultural sector emissions and low-carbon farming (Source: **10568/124664**).

CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, food security, and adaptive land management** in **Burkina Faso** through integrated interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **IFPRI and partners** assessed **poultry value chain development** as a nutrition-sensitive adaptation strategy, linking water use, hygiene, and women’s empowerment (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE, doi:10.7910/DVN/X7RRHL).

- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **gender gaps in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption**, addressing drought tolerance and institutional barriers (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/KQJUEP).

- **CGIAR** explored consumer preferences for low-impact beef to drive eco-efficient market segmentation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF).
- **CGIAR** conducted socioeconomic surveys to characterize sustainable cacao agroforestry systems for forest conservation and peacebuilding (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/G76RMO).

Costa Rica.narrative:
CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program and partners prioritized **climate resilience, policy robustness, and GHG mitigation** in **Costa Rica** through participatory scenario planning, digital emergency systems, and technical advisory services. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** enhanced Costa Rica’s **2020 Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC)** via participatory future scenarios, engaging 350+ stakeholders to strengthen emission reduction and resilience measures (Sources: 10568/121243, 10568/121244).
- **CCAFS and Bioversity International** piloted a **digital emergency response system** in Siquirres, enabling data-driven recovery from Hurricane Otto and informing a USD 57M damage restoration plan (Source: 10568/102482).
- **CCAFS** led the **Central American Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) regional strategy (2018–2030)**, using scenario methodologies to design a policy framework resilient to climate uncertainties, attracting USD 2M in implementation funding (Source: 10568/121429).
- **CCAFS and GRA** hosted a **webinar series** on low-emission development, including Costa Rica-specific analyses of **dairy intensification trade-offs** and **N₂O emissions inventories** (Source: 10568/101906).
- **CCAFS** advised the **Central American Agricultural Council** on **GHG accounting and mitigation practices** for the agricultural sector (Source: 10568/124664).
Costa Rica.Adaptation:
• **CCAFS** developed **participatory future scenarios** to robust Costa Rica’s **2020 NDC**, using stakeholder consultations to refine emission reduction and resilience strategies across all sectors (10568/121243, 10568/121244).
• **CCAFS and Bioversity International** designed a **digital emergency response system** for the Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock (MoAL), enabling real-time data collection on 3,575 households and USD 57M in damages post-Hurricane Otto, thereby improving climate resilience and infrastructure recovery planning (10568/102482).
• **CCAFS** contributed to the **Central American CSA regional strategy (2018–2030)**, applying scenario methodologies to create a policy instrument adaptable to diverse climate futures, securing USD 2M in funding (10568/121429).
Costa Rica.Mitigation:
• **CCAFS** advised the **Central American Agricultural Council** on **GHG inventory methods and mitigation practices**, delivering training to ministries on agricultural sector emissions (10568/124664).
• **CCAFS and GRA** explored **low-emission development pathways** in a webinar series, including Costa Rica-specific analyses of **dairy sector trade-offs** and **N₂O emissions inventories** (10568/101906).
Costa Rica.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Côte d'Ivoire.narrative:
CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program advanced climate-smart agriculture in Côte d’Ivoire through evidence-based adaptation strategies and mitigation planning. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** quantified economic risks of inaction in cocoa production adaptation (Source A).
- **CCAFS** integrated climate risk indicators into financial underwriting for cocoa/coffee supply chains (Source B).
- **CCAFS** co-developed Côte d’Ivoire’s Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan, aligning with NDC commitments (Source C).

• **CCAFS** collaborated with Root Capital to embed climate risk hotspot indicators into loan underwriting for cocoa/coffee producer organizations, facilitating $146M in climate-informed financing (10568/121396).
• **CCAFS** contributed to Côte d’Ivoire’s Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan, designing 12 resilience-building projects to safeguard agricultural productivity against climate threats (10568/106807).
Côte d'Ivoire.Mitigation: • **CCAFS** supported Côte d’Ivoire’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) by incorporating greenhouse gas emission reduction measures into the Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan, despite the country’s minor global emissions profile (10568/106807).

• **CCAFS** integrated **climate change and gender axes** into El Salvador’s **Sectorial Policy of Agriculture (2019–2024)** (10568/124690).

Ethiopia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive agriculture** in **Ethiopia** through evidence-based interventions, policy integration, and technology adoption. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** conducted rigorous impact evaluations of multisectoral graduation models to strengthen food security, livelihoods, and climate resilience under the **Productive Safety Net Programme (PSNP4)** (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).
- **CIMMYT-CCAFS** collaborated with national stakeholders to develop Ethiopia’s **National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy** and built local research capacity (10568/65079).
- **Researchers** synthesized local adaptation practices, drought tolerance, and institutional responses across agro-ecological zones (10568/56997).
- **Climate modeling teams** simulated future crop yields (e.g., maize) under extreme weather scenarios to inform adaptive strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
- **Digital Green and MoA** piloted **video-mediated extension** to accelerate adoption of improved agricultural technologies (doi:10.7910/DVN/OJDSUF).
Ethiopia.Adaptation:
• **IFPRI** evaluated the causal impacts of **multisectoral graduation packages** (livelihoods, nutrition, gender, and climate resilience) on PSNP4 households using clustered RCT designs across Amhara and Oromia regions, measuring outcomes in food security, child nutrition, and women’s empowerment (doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).

• **CIMMYT-CCAFS** partnered with **Ethiopian Institute of Agricultural Research (EIAR)** to integrate climate research into Ethiopia’s **National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy**, enhancing national research capacity (10568/65079).

• **Researchers** documented **local adaptation practices** (e.g., drought management, flood responses) and institutional vulnerabilities in nine districts, linking climate change to resource degradation and population growth (10568/56997).

• **Climate scientists** generated **high-resolution projections** (temperature, precipitation, extreme events) to simulate impacts on maize yields and inform sustainable agriculture strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).

• **CGIAR** reviewed **policy frameworks** for climate-smart agriculture, highlighting gaps in productivity and technology adoption among smallholder farmers (10568/108473).

• **Digital Green and Ethiopian MoA** assessed a **video-mediated extension program** to improve farmers’ knowledge and adoption of improved agricultural practices (doi:10.7910/DVN/OJDSUF).
Ethiopia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Ethiopia.Water:
• **Bioversity/CGIAR** developed **forage suitability maps** for Ethiopia using GIS tools, integrating climate (precipitation, temperature, elevation) and soil data to identify optimal growing conditions (doi:10.7910/DVN/FNEGDP).

• **NMA and IRI** enhanced **climate data tools** for meteorological agencies to analyze spatial-temporal patterns of droughts, floods, and dekadal rainfall, supporting socio-economic decision-making (10568/116486).

• **CCAFS** conducted **panel surveys (2018–2021)** on 1,511 households in Guatemala/Honduras, assessing sociodemographic, climate service access, and empowerment dimensions (Source: *doi:10.7910/DVN/OWML6C*).

• **CCAFS** characterized **rural households** in the Trifinio region (2015) to baseline climate adaptation interventions for common bean cultivation (Source: *doi:10.7910/DVN/PA2BCY*).

• **CCAFS** deployed a **community-based climate risk surveillance system** in Chiquimula’s Dry Corridor, supporting 7,500 households with drought/food-security data for municipal councils (Source: *10568/121412*).

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, water resource planning, and adaptation strategies** in **Honduras** through data-driven tools, climate modeling, and field-based monitoring. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed high-resolution climate surfaces and future scenarios to support policy analysis (Sources: *doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ*, *doi:10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB*).
- **CCAFS** implemented Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) monitoring frameworks in villages like Santa Rita to track adoption, food security, and climate vulnerability (Sources: *doi:10.7910/DVN/OSNTKT*, *doi:10.7910/DVN/OWML6C*).

• **CIAT** generated **historical and future climate surfaces** (precipitation, temperature, solar radiation) at 1 km² resolution to inform **decision-making for climate change adaptation**, including seasonal and annual climate normals for policy analysis (doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ).

• **CIAT** developed **future climate change scenarios** (2030s–2080s) for Honduras using CMIP5 projections, providing **spatialized anomalies** for precipitation, temperature, and solar radiation to support **long-term adaptation planning** (doi:10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB).

• **CGIAR** identified **high-impact climate change spots** in Honduras (and neighboring countries) using **DSSAT yield simulations**, classifying **hot spots, adaptation spots, and pressure zones** for drybean production (doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).

• **CGIAR/CCAFS** piloted **drought index insurance** in collaboration with IRI and SAG to **transfer productive risks** for marginalized farmers, enabling investment in climate-resilient technologies (10568/93218).

Indonesia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural solutions in Indonesia** through targeted research on **water productivity, pest management, and mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** enhanced **rodent management in rice-based systems** via community-driven campaigns, improving yields by **0.7 t/ha** through **Ecologically Based Rodent Management (EBRM)** policies (doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).
- **Researchers at CCAFS** modeled **supplemental irrigation and timely planting** to boost rainfed rice productivity in Central Java, identifying **water productivity gaps** and adaptation strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/22798).
- **CGIAR supported Indonesia’s MRV systems** to improve **greenhouse gas (GHG) monitoring** in agriculture, enabling evidence-based **NDC mitigation targets** (10568/121260).
Indonesia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** evaluated **media campaigns** to promote **Ecologically Based Rodent Management (EBRM)** in rice agriculture, achieving **knowledge/attitude shifts** and **0.7 t/ha yield gains** through community engagement (doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).

• **Researchers used modeling (ORYZA2000)** to assess **rainfed rice yield constraints** in Central Java, demonstrating that **timely planting, supplemental irrigation, and deep tillage** could stabilize yields (doi:10.7910/DVN/22798).

• **CGIAR facilitated stakeholder consultations** to develop a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** for APEC economies, including Indonesia (10568/121197).

• **CCAFS provided climate risk data** to **Root Capital**, enabling **loan underwriting** for coffee/cocoa producers based on **climate hotspot indicators** (10568/121396).

• **CGIAR organized regional conferences** (e.g., Hanoi 2018) to strengthen **climate-resilient R4D collaborations** in Southeast Asia (10568/101149).
Indonesia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR assisted Indonesia in improving agricultural MRV systems** to track **GHG emissions (1.4–1.9 GtCO₂/yr)**, supporting **NDC mitigation targets** and transparency in livestock-related emissions (10568/121260).
Indonesia.Water:
• **CGIAR researchers analyzed daily precipitation/temperature data (2000–2018)** to link **climate variability** with **drought/heat vulnerability** in Indonesia’s sub-regions (doi:10.7910/DVN/2LRIOU).

• **APSIM modeling advancements** enabled simulations of **water use efficiency** and **anaerobic/aerobic soil transitions** in rice-based systems, addressing **water shortages** and future resource constraints (doi:10.7910/DVN/23782).

- The Center guided the **World Bank’s Carbon Asset Facility for Agriculture**, developing **soil carbon crediting and monitoring frameworks** to inform the **USD 500M Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Development Program for Results** (Source: 10568/121203).

Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, policy integration, and sector-specific innovations** in **Kenya** through evidence-based interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated climate-smart soil practices to enhance soil fertility, reduce erosion, and stabilize crop/livestock production in western Kenya (doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).
- **CCAFS** aligned national policies with climate adaptation, food security, and mitigation co-benefits through institutional coordination (10568/110582).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate risk profiles across 45 counties to prioritize value chains for resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/MCGKE4).
Kenya.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted a cost-benefit analysis of **8 climate-smart soil practices** (e.g., agroforestry, liming) to improve soil nitrogen cycles, reduce erosion, and stabilize crop/livestock production in Bungoma, Kakamega, and Siaya Counties (doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

• **CGIAR** developed **index-based insurance-linked credit products** (Risk Contingent Credit) in Machakos County, integrating rainfall-triggered payouts to enhance food security and shock resilience among smallholder farmers (doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).

• **CCAFS** reviewed **Kenya’s climate-agriculture policies**, highlighting gaps in mitigation focus while emphasizing adaptation co-benefits (e.g., productivity gains) and aligning frameworks with SDGs/CAADP (10568/110582).

• **CGIAR** generated **county-level climate risk profiles** (2016–2021) for 45 rural counties, prioritizing value chains (e.g., maize, dairy) based on resilience, food security, and poverty impacts (doi:10.7910/DVN/MCGKE4).

• **CGIAR** explored **digital tools for fisheries adaptation** in coastal Kenya, assessing access to weather forecasts and climate data among 2,137 fisherfolk to improve management (doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR).

• **CCAFS** supported **policy development** in East Africa by providing climate vulnerability/risk data to inform national/local strategies (10568/78781).
Kenya.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-smart dairy practices** as Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs), linking feed efficiency and livestock management to **greenhouse gas reductions** (14% of Kenya’s emissions) while boosting resilience (10568/67901).

• **CGIAR** mapped **current/future forage suitability** in Kenya using GIS tools, integrating precipitation, temperature, and soil data to guide climate-adaptive land use (doi:10.7910/DVN/FNEGDP).

• **CIAT** included **soil conservation** (e.g., erosion reduction) as a core criterion for climate-smart soil practices, linking water retention to productivity (doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

- The **Digital Fisheries Information System (FIS)** under **PROFISHBLUE** identified climate risks in aquaculture and fisheries, developing climate-informed decision support for fish farmers and artisanal fishers (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD, doi:10.7910/DVN/IGKCNP).
- CGIAR’s **climate information services** scaled participatory tools to empower farmers in on-farm decision-making (Source: 10568/56840).

• **PROFISHBLUE (SADC)** developed a **Digital Fisheries Information System (FIS)** to assess climate risks in aquaculture and fisheries, documenting adaptation strategies and weather forecast use among 639 Malawian fish farmers and artisanal fishers (doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD, doi:10.7910/DVN/IGKCNP).

Nicaragua.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and policy integration** in **Nicaragua** through participatory research, vulnerability assessments, and supply-chain adaptation. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led farmer-driven crop variety evaluations for climate adaptation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
- **CIAT** informed the **National Adaptation Plan for Agriculture**, securing USD 24.12M for coffee/cocoa smallholder resilience (Source: 10568/35599).
Nicaragua.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** enabled farmer participation in **tricot trials** (triadic variety comparisons) to evaluate climate-adaptive common bean and wheat varieties, linking observations to environmental data for yield performance analysis (doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CIAT** analyzed **40 years of climate-trend data** to quantify yield declines (–5%/decade for beans, –4% for maize) due to deforestation-linked warming and rainfall shifts, informing government policies like bean export controls and crop diversification (doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

• **CIAT** contributed to Nicaragua’s **National Adaptation Plan**, prioritizing smallholder coffee/cocoa livelihoods and leveraging USD 24.12M in IFAD investments for climate-proofed inputs and weather information systems (10568/35599).

• **CGIAR** conducted **Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessments (CRVA)** to map sensitivity, exposure, and adaptive capacity at sub-national scales, identifying high-impact zones for drybeans (doi:10.7910/DVN/O8GOHP).

• **CGIAR** simulated **spatial yield changes** using DSSAT to classify climate hotspots, adaptation spots, and pressure zones for drybeans across Central America, including Nicaragua (doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **soil conservation strategies** in Matagalpa using the **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)**, quantifying erosion mitigation potential from crop/vegetation management (doi:10.7910/DVN/JJ6VZ6).

• **CGIAR** surveyed **180 Nicaraguan farmers** on perceptions of climate risks, CSA practice costs/benefits, and socio-economic barriers to adoption (doi:10.7910/DVN/6UYI0X).
Nicaragua.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** measured **nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions** from urine patches in degraded/improved pastures, providing country-specific emission factors for GHG mitigation in Nicaragua (doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ).

• **CGIAR** quantified **GHG mitigation potential** in El Tuma La Dalia (Nicaragua) using the **Cool Farm Tool**, assessing soil, cropping systems, and livestock data for carbon calculator inputs (doi:10.7910/DVN/FLJLMC).
Nicaragua.Water:
• **CGIAR** demonstrated that **maize is more water-limited than beans** due to its longer growing season, with deforestation exacerbating rainfall frequency declines and earlier rainy-season ends (doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

• **CGIAR** integrated **soil water erosion analysis** into adaptation strategies, using USLE factors (crop management, conservation practices) to evaluate long-term resilience in hilly landscapes (doi:10.7910/DVN/JJ6VZ6).

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Niger** through evidence-based frameworks, climate-smart agriculture (CSA) monitoring, and climate risk profiling. Specifically:
- **CIAT/IWMI/IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **CCAFS** developed an **Integrated Monitoring Framework for Climate-Smart Agriculture** in the **Fakara Climate-Smart Village**, tracking adoption of CSA practices, climate information access, and gender-disaggregated impacts on livelihoods (doi:10.7910/DVN/HVUN1U).
Niger.Adaptation:

• **CCAFS** implemented **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) monitoring** in Niger’s Fakara Village, assessing adoption of adaptive practices, climate information services, and their effects on food security and adaptive capacity (doi:10.7910/DVN/HVUN1U).

• **CGIAR researchers** compiled **climate risk profiles** for Niger under the **World Food Program**, analyzing precipitation and temperature hazards to inform climate adaptation strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).

• **CCAFS** developed **Country CSA Profiles for Niger**, guiding mainstreaming of climate-smart practices into national agricultural investment plans (10568/121246).

• **CGIAR** integrated **household food security surveys** in Niger (2018–2023), linking coping strategies to **climate hazards (e.g., extreme weather, rainy season performance)** and conflict shocks (doi:10.7910/DVN/SWJOEN).

• **CGIAR’s AICCRA project** analyzed **climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses** in Niger’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) agricultural adaptation projects, aligning with the **IPCC Risk Framework** (doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).
Niger.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** strengthened **African climate negotiators’ capacity** in attribution science, linking **greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes**—critical for mitigation negotiations (10568/178443).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced **forages and pastures** through **germplasm adaptation, agronomic trait selection, and climate-resilient production systems**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated pasture germplasm adaptation across tropical ecosystems to identify high-productivity, persistent forages under grazing systems (Source A).
- **CIAT/CORPOICA** selected Brachiaria accessions with superior traits and tested their yield resilience to climate and soil variability in Colombia (Source B).
- **CIAT** analyzed genotype-environment interactions in *Desmodium ovalifolium* to quantify soil fertility and climate effects on forage quality (Source C).
- **ICARDA/ILRI** assessed land suitability and supplemental irrigation needs for alfalfa production under climate change in Ethiopia (Source D).
forages and pastures.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** established the **RIEPT Network** to evaluate pasture germplasm adaptation in semievergreen tropical forests, identifying high-productivity forages under grazing management systems (doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
• **CIAT/CORPOICA** developed the **Colombian Brachiaria Network** to select and test accessions with superior agronomic traits, assessing climate and soil effects on yield across 11 sites (doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
• **CIAT** conducted multilocational trials on *Desmodium ovalifolium* to quantify environmental influences (soil fertility/climate) on tannin content and forage quality (doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
forages and pastures.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR supported **climate-resilient agricultural systems in Burkina Faso** through **water access and crop diversification**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers facilitated microfinance-enabled small-scale irrigation** (Source: 10568/75061).
- **The Center promoted vegetable-based crop diversification** to sustain livelihoods during dry seasons (Source: 10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.Adaptation: • CGIAR **enabled crop diversification (vegetables) in Burkina Faso** to extend productivity into dry seasons, improving food security and income for smallholder farmers (10568/75061).

fruits and vegetables.Water: • CGIAR **supported smallholder access to water** via microfinance-backed pumps, drawing from nearby dams to sustain vegetable production (10568/75061).

general / cross-cutting.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **cross-cutting climate resilience and agrifood systems innovation** through large-scale evidence synthesis and integrated regional assessments. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) initiative** synthesized 2,916 studies across Africa to evaluate climate-resilient agronomic practices, soil fertility management, and intercropping strategies, enabling policy design for sustainable intensification (doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
- **The Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia (TAFSSA) initiative** conducted district-level assessments in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal to link climate risk responses, water access, and gender-inclusive natural resource management with livelihood outcomes (doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).
general / cross-cutting.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA)** compiled 112,859 observations from 2,916 studies to assess climate-resilient agronomy, soil fertility, and intercropping in Africa, supporting policy and program investments (doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).

• **The SELEVER study** evaluated an integrated poultry value chain, women’s empowerment, and hygiene interventions in Burkina Faso to improve nutritional resilience and adaptation through behavior change (doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE).

• **TAFSSA (South Asia)** assessed climate risk responses, agricultural land use, and gender roles in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal to enhance adaptive livelihood strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).

• **SPIR (Ethiopia)** implemented a multisectoral graduation model to strengthen food security, child nutrition, and climate resilience in PSNP4 households via randomized controlled trials (10568/174179).

• **IFPRI** reviewed climate change impacts on agrifood systems and evaluated policy options for climate-smart agriculture and resilience (10568/174179).
general / cross-cutting.Mitigation: • **IFPRI** analyzed climate change mitigation strategies within agrifood systems, modeling policy interventions for low-emission agriculture (10568/174179).
general / cross-cutting.Water:
• **TAFSSA (South Asia)** integrated water access assessments into district-level agrifood systems evaluations, linking it to climate risk responses and natural resource management (doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).

• **SELEVER study** addressed water-related hygiene and poultry production systems as part of its adaptation framework in Burkina Faso (doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers—including **CIAT**, **CIP**, and partners—advanced legume resilience and productivity through **climate-adaptive breeding, participatory variety evaluation, and agroecological assessments** across Africa, Latin America, and Asia. Specifically:
- **CIAT** and partners developed **climate-resilient germplasm** for common beans via genetic mapping under drought (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).
- **CIAT** and **CIP** led **citizen-science trials** (e.g., *tricot* evaluations) to assess farmer-preferred varieties in Nicaragua, Ethiopia, and India (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
- **CIAT** and **ILCA** evaluated **forage legumes** for adaptability in West/Central Africa’s humid/sub-humid zones (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/AGGBVZ).
legumes.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** and partners conducted **multi-location trials** (e.g., *RABAOC* network) to identify forage legumes adapted to soil, climate, and biotic stresses in West/Central Africa, supporting livestock feed systems (doi:10.7910/DVN/AGGBVZ).

• **CIAT** and **CIP** engaged farmers in **crowdsourced variety evaluations** (*tricot* trials) across Nicaragua, Ethiopia, and India to assess yield and performance under local conditions, linking observations to environmental data (doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CIAT** mapped **drought-resilient traits** (e.g., yield, iron/zinc content) in a **MAGIC population of common beans** (Phaseolus vulgaris) via genetic and phenotypic analysis in Colombia, enabling climate-smart breeding (doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).

• **CIAT** and **PABRA** assessed **high-impact climate spots** for drybeans in Central America using DSSAT models, classifying yield vulnerabilities and adaptation priorities (doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).

• **CIAT** and **ISABU** evaluated **improved bean varieties’ impact** on smallholder resilience in Burundi, linking adoption to yield gains and food security under agro-climatic shocks (doi:10.7910/DVN/UMR2OC).

• **CIAT** analyzed **gender constraints** in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption among Tanzanian bean farmers, highlighting disparities in access to technologies and decision-making (doi:10.7910/DVN/LSP40V).

• **CIAT** and **Malawi’s MSP project** accelerated adoption of **market-demanded bean varieties** via participatory platforms, addressing soil/climatic suitability in Dedza, Mzimba, and Kasungu districts (doi:10.7910/DVN/MHZQV0).

• **CGIAR (SPIA)** documented **variety adoption trends** across Sub-Saharan Africa, linking improved beans to income growth and resilience for rural households (doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).

• **CIAT** surveyed **climate vulnerability determinants** among Colombian bush bean farmers, integrating socioeconomic and perception data to inform adaptive strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/E5UYJE).
legumes.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
legumes.Water: • **CIAT** tested **climate-smart agricultural (CSA) practices** in maize-legume systems (e.g., pigeon pea, beans) to improve soil water infiltration, moisture retention, and productivity under variable weather in Tanzania’s Babati region (doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).

livestock (dairy).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart and low-emission dairy systems** globally through **adaptation strategies** in East Africa and **mitigation innovations** in China, Kenya, and the Netherlands. Specifically:
- **ILRI** advanced **farmer-led climate adaptation** in Kenya’s dairy sector (Source_1).
- **CCAFS** led **GHG mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China and **carbon footprint assessments** under Chinese conditions (Source_2).
livestock (dairy).Adaptation:
• **ILRI** documented **Climate-Smart Dairy (CSD) systems** through socio-economic baselines in East Africa, emphasizing improved forages and feeding strategies for resilience in Tanzania and Rwanda (doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW).
• **ILRI** supported **50 pioneer adaptation farmers** in Kenya, promoting locally developed, farmer-led solutions to climate change in dairy through peer-to-peer networks (10568/175700).
livestock (dairy).Mitigation:
• **CCAFS** developed **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China, covering feed and manure management strategies (10568/119591).
• **CCAFS** facilitated **Kenya’s USD 223M NAMA investment** in dairy, leveraging research to secure Green Climate Fund support for GHG reduction (10568/106306).
• **CCAFS/CAAS/CAU/WUR** conducted **carbon footprint assessments** and mitigation options for dairy under Chinese conditions, collaborating with the Sino-Dutch Dairy Development Centre (10568/99443).
• **Wageningen UR (via CCAFS)** shared **Dutch dairy mitigation strategies** as part of the Chinese carbon footprint project (10568/99446).
• **CCAFS** explored **low-emission development options** in large-scale dairy farms, drawing on U.S. experiences for piloting in China (10568/110054).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced livestock and beef systems through **multi-dimensional environmental assessments** and **climate-informed innovations**. Specifically:
- **ILRI (International Livestock Research Institute)** designed **CLEANED**—a rapid ex-ante tool to evaluate livestock value chains’ impacts on **land use, water, GHG emissions, and soil health** (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/4EB5XT, doi:10.7910/DVN/G0G8IY, doi:10.7910/DVN/QIUJM5).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** explored **consumer-driven markets for low-carbon beef** via improved forages and eco-efficiency segmentation (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF, doi:10.7910/DVN/CJQWGU).
- **ILRI** integrated **DHS-GIS datasets** to link livestock systems with **climate, soil, and agroecological variables** for adaptation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/OQIPRW).
livestock (general/beef).Adaptation:
• **ILRI** developed **CLEANED** (v1.0.1–3.0.1), a tool modeling livestock system changes to assess **climate resilience** in land use, productivity, and soil health (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/4EB5XT, doi:10.7910/DVN/G0G8IY, doi:10.7910/DVN/QIUJM5).
• **ILRI** projected **rangeland ecosystem shifts** (2020–2050) under CMIP6 climate forecasts, informing **pastoral adaptation** in the Global South (Source: 10568/172689).
• **ILRI** curated **DHS-GIS databases** linking livestock with **climate (temperature, rainfall), soil, and pasture data** for agroecological adaptation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/OQIPRW).
• **ILRI** reviewed **digital tools** for **climate-informed agroecological transitions** in Brazil’s beef sector, prioritizing **farmer co-creation** and adaptive practices (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/DXPZ8G).
• **ILRI** highlighted **drought impacts on pastoral communities**, advocating for **region-specific climate policies** (Source: 10568/180602).

• **CIAT** analyzed **consumer willingness to pay (WTP)** for **low-carbon beef** produced via improved forages, identifying market segments for **eco-efficient beef** (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF, doi:10.7910/DVN/CJQWGU).
• **ILRI** projected **soil carbon and vegetation cover changes** in rangelands to support **low-carbon livestock policies** (Source: 10568/172689).

• **ILRI** integrated **DHS-GIS datasets** with **rainfall, groundwater, and pasture variables** to model **water-climate-livestock interactions** (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/OQIPRW).
• **ILRI** addressed **drought-induced water scarcity** in pastoral systems, proposing **adaptive water management** for springs and groundwater (Source: 10568/180602).

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient and low-emission livestock systems** across Africa, Asia, and Latin America through **data-driven interventions, policy engagement, and technical innovation**. Specifically:
- **ILRI and partners** conducted **nationally representative household surveys** in Pakistan to analyze **agricultural water use and climate adaptation** in mixed crop-livestock systems (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA).
- **ICRAF, ILRI, and Heifer International** scaled **climate-smart dairy practices** in Kenya via **Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs)**, linking mitigation finance to sustainable feed and manure management (Source: 10568/67901).
- **CIAT and Bioversity** advanced **forage nutritional assessment** using **near-infrared spectroscopy** to enhance livestock productivity in Colombia (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/XPNIQY).
livestock (general/small ruminants).Adaptation:
• **ILRI and CCAFS** documented **land-use/cover changes** in Ethiopia’s Gudo Beret Watershed, quantifying **feed resource competition** and **grazing land degradation** to inform **sustainable land management** (doi:10.7910/DVN/8IXPXX).
• **ILRI and partners** collected **baseline data on climate change knowledge** among Colombian dairy producers, assessing **adoption of improved forages and conservation practices** in Cauca’s Patía and Mercaderes municipalities (doi:10.7910/DVN/SMBVYF).
• **ILRI and CCAFS** facilitated **learning journeys** in Kenya and Tanzania to **enhance farmers’ adaptive capacity** through climate-smart agriculture options (10568/77047).
• **ILRI and partners** analyzed **land-cover shifts** in Zimbabwe’s Mid Zambezi Valley, linking **declining cotton profitability** to **expanded livestock grazing** and **human-wildlife conflict dynamics** (doi:10.7910/DVN/VMVZRD).
livestock (general/small ruminants).Mitigation:
• **ILRI and CCAFS** conducted a **meta-analysis of climate-smart dairy practices** in Kenya, aligning **feed management innovations** with **Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs)** to reduce GHG emissions (10568/67901).
• **ILRI, ICRAF, and Heifer International** integrated **climate-smart agriculture** into the **East African Dairy Development (EADD) program**, demonstrating **methane reduction** via **fodder banks, legumes, and manure management** (10568/35596).
• **CCAFS** provided **technical guidance** to the **World Bank and ADB** on **carbon crediting for soil carbon** and **low-emissions livestock production**, informing a **$500M Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Program** (10568/121203).
• **ILRI and CCAFS** developed **GHG measurement tools** and engaged with **Kenya’s Climate Change Act (2016)** to support **regional mitigation inventories** (10568/77047).
• **CCAFS** framed **climate-smart agriculture** as a **triple-win strategy**—boosting productivity, resilience, and **carbon sequestration**—while addressing **25% of global GHG emissions** from agriculture (10568/100181).

- The Center developed **Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies** to estimate intake and enteric methane emission factors for smallholder sheep systems, integrating live weight measurements, feed quality data, and seasonal digestibility assessments (doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient maize systems** through **digital agriculture, adaptive farming practices, and soil health interventions**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** deployed **sensor-based climate and soil monitoring** in Guatemala to optimize maize productivity via digital technologies (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7).
- **CIMMYT** simulated **climate change impacts on maize yields** in Ethiopia, providing adaptive strategies for sustainable agriculture (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
maize.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** developed **climate-smart agricultural (CSA) practices** for maize-legume systems, assessing soil moisture, infiltration, and physiological traits under variable weather (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).
• **CIAT** analyzed **climate risks in maize supply chains** in Colombia, proposing adaptation measures to reduce vulnerability (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/G9ADYV).
• **CGIAR** evaluated **conservation agriculture (CA) systems** in Zambia, demonstrating yield and economic benefits from no-till/dibblestick CA with maize-legume rotations (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/RSGLGB).
• **CIAT** studied **yield variation factors** in Colombian maize crops, linking climate, soil, and agronomic management to resilience (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/LSDQ5C).
• **CGIAR** tested **ratooning and crop rotation** in Southern Africa, enhancing drought tolerance in maize-pigeonpea systems (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/EQZR5C).
• **CGIAR** documented **historical climate trends** in Nicaragua, highlighting maize’s water limitations and adaptation responses like area expansion (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).
maize.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** assessed **soil organic carbon dynamics** in Tanzania’s Usambara Mountains, informing climate-smart interventions for maize cultivation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/1YZAQZ).
maize.Water:

• **CGIAR** measured **soil water infiltration** and seasonal moisture trends in Tanzania and Babati (Tanzania), supporting CSA practices (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES, doi:10.7910/DVN/1YZAQZ).

rice.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized climate-resilient rice systems through **adaptation planning, water-efficient technologies, and GHG mitigation strategies**, particularly in **Vietnam’s Mekong River Delta and Southeast Asia**. Specifically:
- **IRRI and CCAFS** developed **participatory climate-smart adaptation plans** for rice, integrating risk mapping, hydrological management, and gender-inclusive strategies (Sources: 10568/76928, 10568/100093, 10568/115806, 10568/111168).
- **CCAFS and national partners** mapped **drought, salinity, and flooding risks** to optimize rice production calendars under extreme and normal climate scenarios (Sources: 10568/100093, 10568/115802).
- **IRRI and ICON** explored **non-flooded cropping systems** to reduce water use while monitoring disease dynamics linked to water management shifts (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).
- **CGIAR’s global datasets** projected **yield changes under climate models** (ISIMIP3b/CMIP6) to inform CO₂ fertilization and mitigation pathways (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
rice.Adaptation:
• **IRRI and CCAFS** conducted **gender-disaggregated surveys** in Vietnam’s Mekong Delta to identify climate perception gaps and adaptation strategies among male/female rice farmers (Source: 10568/76928).

• **CCAFS and Vietnam’s Ministry of Agriculture** developed **Climate-Smart Maps and Adaptation Plans (CS MAP)** for 13 Mekong Delta provinces, integrating **flood/salinity risk maps**, adaptive rice calendars, and stakeholder consultations to harmonize water/labor policies (Source: 10568/100093).

• **IRRI and CCAFS** updated **regional risk maps** for drought/salinity in rice areas, aligning adaptation plans with climate-smart agriculture frameworks (Source: 10568/111168).

• **CCAFS and local partners** mapped **climate risks for rice/short-day crops** in **Southern Vietnam and Central Highlands**, using participatory methods to tailor adaptation plans to 3 water-scenario zones (Source: 10568/115802).

• **IRRI (ICON Project)** analyzed **disease progression (e.g., sheath blight)** under shifted water management regimes (flooded→non-flooded), linking crop rotation and water practices to pathogen dynamics (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).

• **Bioversity International** characterized **heat/abiotic stress zones** for rice accessions in Africa, enabling climate-resilient crop characterization (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/LI5D3A).

• **CGIAR’s systematic review** compiled **Site-Specific Nutrient Management (SSNM) data** for rice in Africa/Asia, assessing agronomic efficiency under diverse climatic conditions (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/H23MVL).
rice.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR (via DSSAT modeling)** projected **rice yield changes under SSP370/SSP585 scenarios**, evaluating CO₂ fertilization effects and GHG-driven climate trajectories (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).

• **IRRI developed MapAWD**, an **Excel-based tool** to assess **bio-physical suitability of Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD)** for **GHG mitigation projects**, using spatially explicit water balance models (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/DUMAKG).
rice.Water:
• **CCAFS and Vietnam’s Ministry of Agriculture** integrated **hydrological data, local knowledge, and infrastructure maps** to design **adaptive water-sharing schemes** for rice, addressing flooding/salinity risks (Source: 10568/100093).

• **IRRI (ICON Project)** studied **water management shifts** (flooded→non-flooded) in rice-maize rotations, analyzing **water/nitrogen budgets** and disease risks (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).

• **IRRI developed MapAWD** to identify **climatically favorable zones for AWD**, a **water-saving technique** applicable to GHG mitigation (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/DUMAKG).

• **CCAFS mapped water scarcity risks** for rice in **Southern Vietnam/Central Highlands**, aligning adaptation plans with 3 water-scenario models (Source: 10568/115802).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR’s International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) advanced **roots and tubers research**—particularly cassava—through **climate-resilient breeding, genetic evaluation, and edaphoclimatic zone-specific adaptation**. Specifically:
- CIAT mapped **global climate suitability zones** for cassava cultivation, integrating temperature, seasonality, and dry-season constraints (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/WFAMUM).
- CIAT systematically evaluated **germplasm resistance** across diverse edaphoclimatic zones (e.g., Medium Altitude Tropics, Semi-Arid, High Altitude) to identify traits for pest/disease resilience and yield potential (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/KM8CQG, doi:10.7910/DVN/WXIGQY, doi:10.7910/DVN/EOIB2X).
- CIAT implemented **recurrent selection programs** tailored to ecosystem-specific challenges (e.g., acid soils, drought, high-altitude cool climates) to enhance trait resilience (Sources: doi:10.7910/DVN/FBX5DH, doi:10.7910/DVN/1K4GCI, doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE, doi:10.7910/DVN/30PHOU, doi:10.7910/DVN/OEQAKM, doi:10.7910/DVN/U2OOTE).
roots and tubers.Adaptation:
• **CIAT developed climate suitability maps** for cassava, defining cultivation zones based on growing season temperature, dry-season months, and temperature range to guide adaptive farming (doi:10.7910/DVN/WFAMUM).

• **CIAT evaluated germplasm diversity** in **Edaphoclimatic Zones 3–5** (Lowland Tropics, Medium Altitude Tropics, High Altitude), characterizing resistance to pests/diseases (e.g., CMD, anthracnose, Cercospora complexes) and physiological traits like temperature/photoperiod sensitivity (doi:10.7910/DVN/KM8CQG, doi:10.7910/DVN/WXIGQY, doi:10.7910/DVN/EOIB2X).

• **CIAT implemented zone-specific breeding trials** (Zones 1–7) with recurrent selection programs to target **drought, acid soils, and humidity stress**, prioritizing traits like yield potential and dry matter content alongside ecosystem-specific pest resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/FBX5DH, doi:10.7910/DVN/1K4GCI, doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE, doi:10.7910/DVN/30PHOU, doi:10.7910/DVN/OEQAKM, doi:10.7910/DVN/U2OOTE).
roots and tubers.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
roots and tubers.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient wheat systems** across Asia and Africa through participatory variety testing, yield-enhancing technologies, and food security monitoring. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** deployed **citizen science (tricot trials)** to evaluate wheat varieties under abiotic stress in Ethiopia (durum wheat) and India (bread wheat), linking farmer observations to environmental data for climate-adaptive breeding (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** upscaled **weather-resilient wheat technologies** in India, achieving **50% yield increases** despite projected climate-induced losses (6–23% by 2050) through farmer-led adaptation in Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, and Uttar Pradesh (Source: 10568/109732).
wheat.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** facilitated **farmer-led tricot trials** in Ethiopia (62 durum wheat varieties) and India (21 bread wheat varieties) to assess climate-adaptive performance, integrating geographic and environmental data for variety selection (doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** promoted **weather-resilient practices** in India, mitigating yield losses from hailstorms, temperature extremes, and excess rainfall while achieving **50% yield gains** through improved technologies in high-risk districts (10568/109732).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** upscaled **climate-adapted wheat varieties** (alongside sorghum, barley, and millets) in Ethiopia, Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania, with **4,000+ smallholder farmers** adopting best-performing lines to strengthen livelihood resilience (10568/121262).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed the **Regional Agriculture Forecasting Tool (CRAFT)** to monitor pre-harvest wheat production in Nepal, supporting food security during crises (Earthquake-2015, COVID-2020) and federal restructuring (10568/121357).
